news_summary: Researchers have made significant progress in uncovering the origins and purpose of the ancient giant stone jars of Laos, shedding new light on this archaeological mystery.
news_summary_fr: Les chercheurs ont réalisé des progrès significatifs dans la mise au jour des origines et de la fonction des anciennes jarres géantes en pierre du Laos, apportant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur ce mystère archéologique.
news_summary_es: Los investigadores han logrado avances significativos en el esclarecimiento de los orígenes y la finalidad de las antiguas vasijas de piedra gigantes de Laos, lo que arroja nueva luz sobre este misterio arqueológico.

articles.title: Mystery of the ancient giant stone jars of Laos may have been solved
articles.summary: In central Laos, the landscape is littered with enormous stone jars, some 3 metres high, and we may be closer to understanding how and when they were used
articles.content:
In central Laos, the landscape is littered with enormous stone jars, some 3 metres high, and we may be closer to understanding how and when they were used

The plain of jars in Laos Alvov/Shutterstock

The remains of at least 37 people have been found interred in a giant stone jar in Laos, reshaping our understanding of one of South-East Asia’s most puzzling ancient landscapes.

Around the remote Xieng Khouang plateau in central Laos sit thousands of giant stone jars, some 3 metres high and weighing several tonnes. The Plain of Jars has long been thought to be an ancient megalithic site, but who made the jars and what they were used for have remained mysterious.

“There are all these old stories associated with them, that they were made for giants who used them for brewing rice wine,” says Nick Skopal at James Cook University in Australia.

Advertisement

Investigations in the 1930s led to suggestions that the jars were associated with the South-East Asian Iron Age between about 500 BC and AD 500 and were used to cremate or decompose bodies. More recent studies have found glass beads, jewellery and a few cremated remains, as well as burials near the jars but not within them.

Now, Skopal and his colleagues have found the densely packed remains of many people in their excavation of a jar measuring 1.3 metres high and more than 2 metres wide near the Laotian town of Phonsavan. The jar contained the right femurs and skulls from 19 individuals, but teeth from 37 people.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

Radiocarbon dating of samples showed that the remains were deposited in multiple phases over up to 270 years, between the 9th and 12th centuries AD.

The remains were neatly packed in, potentially after an initial period of decomposition elsewhere, with the longer bones laid out towards the edges, and many smaller, more fragile bones missing.

“This is an incredibly consequential discovery,” says Nigel Chang, also at James Cook University, who wasn’t involved in the research. “After almost 100 years of speculation, this is the first of these stone jars to be investigated with irrefutable association with mortuary behaviour.”

About 500 metres from the big, primary jar was a group of smaller stone jars, some of which contained glass beads. Skopal suggests that people put dead bodies inside the smaller jars until the flesh deteriorated, then moved the bones to the larger jar.

“Were the stone jars some way for the soul to be released and be prepared for the afterlife as part of ancestor worship?” he says. “We are doing some DNA testing on these remains inside the jar. That will give us an idea of who these people were and how they were related to each other.”

The dating of the samples reveals when this stone jar was being used, but doesn’t show when it was made.

“It seems to be becoming clear that there was a lot of activity around the jar sites in the second half of the first millennium AD or so,” says Chang. “However, my personal opinion is that the jars themselves are older than that: from 2000 or more years ago.”

Unfortunately, you can’t date the jars themselves, says Skopal, but he adds that his team’s dating of artefacts excavated outside the jar matches what’s inside it, which suggests the jar was placed there when the first bodies were put inside. “It’s starting to suggest that it’s more of a medieval culture, and not an Iron Age thing,” he says.

A newly excavated stone jar containing human remains Dr Nicholas Skopal

Skopal thinks that practice was part of ancestral funerary rites that spanned generations. But there is great variance in the stone jars in Laos, he adds, so there were probably different ways of using them within the wider tradition. At some sites, jars are generally upright, and many are empty – perhaps because of looting – while at other sites, there are many jars with shallower or narrower internal cavities that are lying flat. That implies differences in rites between regions or over time, he says.

“It is very likely that numerous cultural groups could have utilised the jars, or the same cultural group used the same jar as a mortuary facility over an extended period of time,” says Tiatoshi Jamir at Nagaland University in India.

Skopal’s team also found iron tools, earthenware, a copper-based bell and glass beads inside the jar. Chemical analysis revealed the beads were produced in South India and Mesopotamia, indicating long-distance travel and trade.

This isn’t unexpected, he says, given that around AD 1000 was a flourishing period in East and South-East Asia, which included the Song Dynasty and Dali Kingdom in China, Cambodia’s Khmer Empire and the Pagan kingdom in what is now Myanmar.

Marco Mitri at North Eastern Hill University in India, who has worked on similar stone jars in north-east India, more than 1000 kilometres away, says archaeology is revealing an extensive cultural tradition.

He suggests a widespread Austroasiatic population engaged in these funerary traditions for hundreds of years, with similar rites still carried on today in India by an Austroasiatic group called the Khasi, who, after cremation, deposit bones in stone boxes called cists.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant archaeological discovery that sheds light on ancient human cultural practices in Laos, resolving a long-standing mystery about the giant stone jars. This discovery has broad historical and cultural significance, enhancing our understanding of human history and ancient civilizations, which is inspiring and intellectually uplifting for the general public.
articles.personality_title: Ancient stone jars in Laos found to hold human remains, revealing old burial practices
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In central Laos, there are thousands of giant stone jars standing on a plateau called the Plain of Jars. People have long wondered who made these huge jars and what they were used for, with guesses ranging from giant rice wine containers to burial sites.

**What happened** – Archaeologists led by Nick Skopal recently excavated one of these stone jars near the town of Phonsavan. Inside, they found the bones of at least 37 people packed together. Radiocarbon dating showed the remains were placed there over about 270 years between the 9th and 12th centuries AD. The bones were carefully arranged, suggesting a special burial process.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it is the first clear proof that these stone jars were used for mortuary, or burial, purposes. Before, experts only had guesses and scattered evidence. Finding so many human remains inside a jar shows how ancient people in this region cared for their dead and practiced ancestor worship. The discovery also links the jars to a later time period than previously thought, around the medieval era, and shows connections to other ancient cultures through items like beads from South India and Mesopotamia.

**What's next step** – Scientists will now study the DNA of the bones to learn who these people were and how they were related. They will also look at other jars to see if burial practices varied across regions or time. Understanding these jars better will help reveal more about the culture and history of ancient Southeast Asia.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, archaeologists have confirmed that Laos’s giant stone jars were used as ancient burial sites, uncovering a long-hidden chapter of human history.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des jarres en pierre géantes au Laos contiennent des restes humains, révélant d’anciennes pratiques funéraires
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans le centre du Laos, des milliers de jarres en pierre géantes se dressent sur une plaine appelée la Plaine des Jarres. Depuis longtemps, on se demande qui a fabriqué ces énormes jarres et à quoi elles servaient, certains pensant qu’elles étaient utilisées par des géants pour faire du vin de riz ou comme lieux de sépulture.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des archéologues dirigés par Nick Skopal ont récemment fouillé une de ces jarres près de la ville de Phonsavan. À l’intérieur, ils ont trouvé les ossements d’au moins 37 personnes entassés ensemble. La datation au carbone montre que ces restes ont été déposés sur environ 270 ans, entre le IXe et le XIIe siècle après J.-C. Les os étaient soigneusement arrangés, ce qui suggère un rituel funéraire particulier.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle prouve pour la première fois que ces jarres servaient à des fins funéraires. Avant, les spécialistes n’avaient que des hypothèses et des preuves dispersées. Trouver autant de restes humains dans une jarre montre comment les anciens habitants de cette région prenaient soin de leurs morts et pratiquaient le culte des ancêtres. La découverte situe aussi l’usage des jarres à une époque plus récente que prévu, autour du Moyen Âge, et révèle des liens avec d’autres cultures anciennes grâce à des objets comme des perles venant d’Inde du Sud et de Mésopotamie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont maintenant étudier l’ADN des os pour savoir qui étaient ces personnes et comment elles étaient liées. Ils examineront aussi d’autres jarres pour comprendre si les pratiques funéraires variaient selon les régions ou les époques. Mieux comprendre ces jarres aidera à dévoiler davantage la culture et l’histoire de l’Asie du Sud-Est ancienne.

**En une phrase** – Pour la première fois, des archéologues ont confirmé que les jarres géantes du Laos étaient utilisées comme sites funéraires anciens, révélant un chapitre longtemps caché de l’histoire humaine.
articles.personality_title_es: Antiguas jaras de piedra en Laos contienen restos humanos, revelando antiguas prácticas funerarias
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En el centro de Laos, hay miles de enormes jaras de piedra sobre una meseta llamada la Llanura de las Jaras. Durante mucho tiempo se ha preguntado quién las hizo y para qué servían, con ideas que iban desde recipientes para hacer vino de arroz para gigantes hasta lugares de entierro.

**Qué pasó** – Arqueólogos liderados por Nick Skopal excavaron recientemente una de estas jaras cerca del pueblo de Phonsavan. Dentro encontraron los huesos de al menos 37 personas apilados. La datación por radiocarbono mostró que los restos fueron depositados durante unos 270 años entre los siglos IX y XII d.C. Los huesos estaban cuidadosamente organizados, lo que sugiere un proceso especial de entierro.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque es la primera prueba clara de que estas jaras se usaban para entierros. Antes, solo había suposiciones y pruebas dispersas. Encontrar tantos restos humanos dentro de una jarra muestra cómo las personas antiguas de esta región cuidaban a sus muertos y practicaban el culto a los antepasados. El descubrimiento también sitúa el uso de las jaras en un período más reciente de lo pensado, alrededor de la Edad Media, y muestra conexiones con otras culturas antiguas a través de objetos como cuentas de vidrio de India del Sur y Mesopotamia.

**Qué sigue** – Los científicos estudiarán el ADN de los huesos para saber quiénes eran estas personas y cómo estaban relacionadas. También examinarán otras jaras para ver si las prácticas funerarias cambiaron según la región o el tiempo. Entender mejor estas jaras ayudará a conocer más sobre la cultura e historia del antiguo sudeste asiático.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Por primera vez, arqueólogos han confirmado que las enormes jaras de piedra de Laos fueron usadas como sitios funerarios antiguos, revelando un capítulo oculto de la historia humana.
articles.image_prompt: A serene ancient Laotian landscape featuring towering, weathered stone jars partially buried in soft earth, surrounded by carefully arranged skeletal bones and delicate glass beads glowing faintly inside the jars, with gentle warm light casting long shadows and subtle hints of distant mountains under a calm sky.
